title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about friendship and social bonds. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Architecture of Closeness
content.subtitle: What science and philosophy tell us about how friendships form
content.text:
We often speak of friendship as though it simply happens — a natural consequence of shared space and repeated contact, something that either develops or doesn't without much conscious effort. [31]But research in social psychology suggests that the experience of closeness is less spontaneous than we imagine, and that the formation of deep friendships follows identifiable patterns related to the quantity and quality of time spent together, the willingness to share personal information, and the presence of perceived reciprocity.[/31]

The sociologist Ray Oldenburg introduced the concept of 'third places' — spaces that are neither home nor workplace — as essential environments for the formation of community bonds. [32]Oldenburg argued that the decline of these spaces in many modern societies — the local pub, the community centre, the informal gathering place — had contributed directly to a rise in social isolation, depriving people of the regular, low-stakes interactions that form the foundation of lasting friendships.[/32]

The digital age has complicated this picture. [33]Social media platforms offer connectivity on an unprecedented scale, allowing people to maintain hundreds of relationships simultaneously. Yet researchers note a consistent discrepancy between the number of digital connections people report and the number of individuals they feel genuinely close to, a gap that does not appear to narrow simply with increased online activity.[/33]

What appears to matter most in the development of genuine friendship is the willingness to be vulnerable. [34]Studies consistently find that relationships deepen significantly when one person shares something personal or difficult and the other responds with attention and care. This dynamic — known as self-disclosure reciprocity — is the mechanism through which acquaintance becomes friendship.[/34]

Adult friendship poses particular challenges in this regard. [35]Children form friendships with relative ease, partly because school environments provide the repetitive contact and shared experience that encourage self-disclosure. Adults, by contrast, often lack these structural supports, and must actively create opportunities for the kinds of sustained interaction that deepen relationships.[/35]

[36]The philosopher Aristotle distinguished between friendships of utility — those based on mutual benefit — friendships of pleasure — based on shared enjoyment — and friendships of virtue, in which each person genuinely desires the good of the other. He argued that only the third type had the depth and durability that most people seek when they talk about true friendship.[/36]

questions.question: What does research in social psychology suggest about the formation of deep friendships?
options: A) It becomes more difficult as people grow older and become more set in their habits.
B) It depends mainly on people repeatedly being in the same place at the same time.
C) It follows recognisable patterns rather than occurring purely by chance or proximity.
D) It is primarily determined by the individual personalities of those involved.
questions.explanation: The text states that deep friendship formation 'follows identifiable patterns' related to time, information-sharing and reciprocity, rather than being purely spontaneous.

questions.question: What was Ray Oldenburg's argument about the decline of 'third places'?
options: A) That their disappearance has reduced the informal interactions that serve as friendship foundations.
B) That the quality of public spaces determines the overall happiness of a community.
C) That people in cities have fewer genuine friendships than those living in rural areas.
D) That public spaces should be designed specifically to encourage conversation between strangers.
questions.explanation: Oldenburg argued the decline of third places had 'contributed directly to a rise in social isolation, depriving people of the regular, low-stakes interactions' that build friendships.

questions.question: What does research suggest about online social connections?
options: A) That social media use actively harms people's ability to form real friendships.
B) That digital friendships are just as deep and meaningful as face-to-face relationships.
C) That young people are significantly better at forming digital friendships than older people.
D) That having many online connections does not necessarily produce genuine closeness.
questions.explanation: There is a 'consistent discrepancy between the number of digital connections people report and the number of individuals they feel genuinely close to,' which online activity does not close.

questions.question: What is self-disclosure reciprocity and why is it important?
options: A) The process by which sharing something personal and receiving a caring response deepens a relationship.
B) The practice of introducing friends to one another to build wider social networks.
C) The ability to share personal information without causing discomfort to the other person.
D) The tendency for friendships formed in childhood to persist naturally into adult life.
questions.explanation: The text describes it as the dynamic through which sharing something difficult, met with care from the other person, is 'the mechanism through which acquaintance becomes friendship.'

questions.question: Why is forming friendships harder for adults than for children?
options: A) Adults are generally less willing to share personal information than children are.
B) Adult lives are simply too busy to allow the long conversations that friendship requires.
C) Adults tend to hold higher expectations of friendship than children do.
D) Adults often lack the repeated contact and shared experiences that naturally deepen relationships.
questions.explanation: Children benefit from school's 'repetitive contact and shared experience,' while adults 'often lack these structural supports' and must create opportunities themselves.

questions.question: According to the final paragraph, what distinguishes Aristotle's highest category of friendship?
options: A) It is more commonly found in ancient societies than in modern complex ones.
B) It is based on shared values and long acquaintance rather than any practical need.
C) It is most often formed during periods of shared hardship or difficulty.
D) It involves each person genuinely wanting what is best for the other.
questions.explanation: In friendships of virtue, 'each person genuinely desires the good of the other,' which Aristotle considered the deepest and most durable type.
